speaker: interviewer
text: --six, Dora Nesselroth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about when you were working in the garden, picking onions, and the soldier stood in front of you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a SS one.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was holding his hand like that. And I says, what's that? He-- he put me [INAUDIBLE] SS. I said, what are you doing here? He said, nothing. I said, what are you doing? He said, I am taking those bad people who didn't behave like they should-- inmates. And I took him to the school, where I used to go to the school. And we make [INAUDIBLE] to shut up. This is what he told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what he told me. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- what can you tell him? I-- I make-- I-- I thought that I don't understand what he's meaning, but shut up. We know that they have-- they killed those people in the school, young people, people from the streets, not only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used to take somebody from the streets, because they are-- [INAUDIBLE]. They were all zealot of undergrounds. They were those-- the right wings. And they were the left wings. They were [INAUDIBLE]. They were different groups, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were there. It's true. They were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and-- and I worked-- oh, well, I start working about-- at the time. I didn't tell you before. It was a foreman who-- a lady was working there, a older lady. And he hold the head like that. And I grab him by his elbow and not let him to hit her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He turn his head to me. And with his hand, he grabbed me. And I had metals in my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working there, and she make a mistakes in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he tried to beat her up, like everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I went there. And I grab him and not let him to beat her up. I was in the-- the control booths. It was a booth like that. And my eyes was terrible. No doctors, no anything. At the beginning, I put some water in it. And-- and the-- and then they gave me a guard from the camp-- [INAUDIBLE]. And he went with me to a doctor. And the doctor said, no, I am not-- for free, I'm not going to do it. And the guard said, if you're not going to do it, I'm going to kill you. So he took out with a magnet my metals from my eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that get into your eye?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he turned back with his hand, he touched my eye, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was something--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- he had something there, because the whole time he works there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the doctor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did it, and he let me go. He took me home, back home, back. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other factories or other places--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you worked at?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was these two places there. The third one, Czestochowa--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: --evacuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in September of 1944. We were going to Czestochowa. And the Red Army was on the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When did you-- what-- for-- what caused you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They select us to go. We go-- around with machine guns, and the camp. And they took some to Germany and some to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- they say, 'They selected us,' who else was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the inmates. About 7,000 went to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, 120 people in a boxcar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This was the worst kind transportation we had. But the SS-- it was in Kielce. I think it was Kielce town where we needed water. We-- we receive only yellow cheese, salted cheese, yellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know that I didn't eat cheese after the war? I didn't touch it until here. I came to the United States. I start tasting it. And it's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't eat those things. And so we needed water. So I went out. We-- we were very lucky. We had opened wagons, not with the roof, but open, you know, those wagons there. So we had a little bit of rain that came in, so we didn't fainted. And so I-- I climb out. And I went to the pump at the station there. And I took a-- a can of-- I brought with us this can what we took for-- for soup, you know, these things. And I have a little water. I brought it back. And I get for another one. And the train start running. And this SS came over and helped me to go back to the train. They were-- they knew that this is the end of the war. In-- in 19-- 1960-- 1995, the 16th of-- of January, the Russian came over. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they liberated our camp. But two hours before, they took away my father, my brother, and the rest of-- of the Jewish inmates there. They took in-- in wagons. And they went to the west.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean-- well, '95--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- '85. Yeah? It was '85?

speaker: interviewer
text: '80? This was at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the end of the war, yeah, in 19-- the end of the war was late in May. The 9th of May was the end of the war in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa. We were liberated before Auschwitz was liberated, before the other-- the other cities were liberated, because they went like a-- a [? sack, ?] put like a [? sack. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you working in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there-- oh, well, there was a nice guy. I was working with-- testing the-- the ammunition also, testing there. And he came to me. He said, what did you do in-- in Skarzysko-Kamienna?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I work in a office. He said, you don't mind if you work the-- eight hours and then another eight hours? And I want you to work-- we're going to make three shift, eight, eight, and eight. Shifts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, three shifts. He made eight, eight, and eight hours is-- so I said, eight hours we're going to work? It's unbelievable. So-- but it was a very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you remember the name of that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same factory. They have-- the name is [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is the name of the-- of the division where we worked. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was about 7,000 people were working there in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was a little better. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The director of the factory was a little human being, you know? Because after the war, they find out that there was a list of the young people to take him away. And he postponed it. He didn't send the list back. And it was in his room-- A list of young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the whole list of young people, the Jewish people. And he didn't send the-- the list. So those people, they didn't survive too. Because the last group, they came in, and they said, we are going walking to the west because the Russian are here. The Russian are here. And those stupid people start walking out. They walk. They walk in the-- during the walking, they died because it was cold, winter, winter in-- and this particular year was very cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very, very cold, a cold winter, terrible winter. And so after that, I used to go to the track-- to the railroad track searching for my father, for my brother. And I used to look in the front of the-- and the back of the railroad-- railroad track. And I used to take those people, looking over and over again. And to-- sometimes I knew, this is not my father. But still I was walking. And-- and turn over those corpse. With such a-- a terrible thing, I remember only one thing. When the war was ended and they came over and they said, you know, you don't have what to wear. And we're going to open the doors of the looting, they think. And I said, I'm not going. I don't want to be like them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're going to be like-- like that, without shoes? And I said, yes, I'm going to be without shoes, but I'm not going. I don't want to be like there. The Stupid thing. I was waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to Czestochowa in middle of the night with my mother, with a few friends there from-- we-- we became friendly very well with some people. And-- and we saw soldiers going back to-- to Germany. And they asked me from where we go. And I said that our camp is burning. And we have to go looking for another place. They said, don't go there. There are the Russians. That's fine. We go where the Russians are. And so we stood for a while and-- waiting for them to march away. And so we started walking again, and again, and again, and again. It was about 3 kilometers from the city, Czestochowa. We came to Czestochowa. We find a building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a military-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a building-- a private building before the Nazis occupied the Poland. It was a-- a normal building, an apartment building. And they converted this to a-- a military hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have-- in every room, they have bunk beds. They have a table with two benches. And they have mattresses with straw. They had this kind of mattresses. And they-- no-- they have only oven. You remember the European ovens? They go up in the corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in the morning, I went to look for food in the cellars. And I found potatoes. They were frozen, sweet, terrible. My mother cooked-- make those potato balls with no fat, with no anything. And we finded also some cans from-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] From [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cucumbers-- not cucumbers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, big one. And she cooked this thing. And she went to the railroad station to feed those people who came back from the camps, or they came back from Russia, from those people. And she shared with them with this soup-- with this-- what she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after a while, we received packages from friends. My aunt, who was here in America, she sent packages to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our cousin who lives in Paris. Both cousins, they were underground during the war. And-- and he sent-- every day I-- we receive a package. It was 2-pounds package. It was one shoe in one package. The next day's was another shoe in the other package.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we receive a dress, something, even a lemon inside, you know? This is vitamins. And so little by little-- packages, a lot of those packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were dressed with-- and another thing. The Jewish Community Center started very early, because Lublin was liberated about almost a year before we were. So they established the Jewish Community Center in Lublin. They sent people out to other cities. And we were very lucky to have them in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we receive our money. And we receive also clothing. And-- and they helped us to find people were-- they're missing or something. And we finded out from people-- where Mother used to feed them-- that they were in-- in [? Terezina, ?] in Czechoslovakia. Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was there. And I went-- everything was for free at that time. I went to bring my brother back home. My mother was crying because the father, and my brother, the [INAUDIBLE] son, the only son. And his name was after her father, who was a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found my-- my-- my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what did-- what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went-- I-- they told me to take with me some cigarettes. The cigarettes were very-- well, very expensive there in-- in Czechoslovakia at that time. So we came in to-- to-- oh, on the border. Oh my god, I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they show me where to go to. I ask him-- I wanted to go to Prague. From Prague, I would know where to go further-- to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know where the-- where I am, in the south, in the back, or the thing. And they gave me a place in the train with some inmates from Czechoslovakia, from-- from Auschwitz. And they have a special wagon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Attached to these wagons, the-- what do you call those wagons, these wagons for transportation, materials or something like that? And this was a wagon specially for them, a sleeping wagon. And they have cocoa. They have white bread. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And what else they have? What I'll-- I couldn't understand. The first time I had white bread, and I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cocoa. Cocoa, I have. OK. I came to Prague. In Prague we had Russians going around. They were guards, just Czech guards. And I asked him to take care of me because the Russians are too close. They're coming too close to me. I don't want them to came close. And one has even money. He-- he said, see, I have money. I said, good for you. You're rich. You're not supposed to be rich. A Russian not supposed to be rich-- [INAUDIBLE] money. This man, when I went to the information desk, they served you also bread and some kind of a marmalade. I didn't want marmalade. I want coffee. And I want a piece of bread. He saw that. And he said, come, I will to show you where the uprising in Prague was. So he show me. And then, later on, he said, I go home, and I will come back. You go and stay close to this lady from this-- I told her that you are afraid of the Russians. Thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- he went home. He brought a sandwich, you know, for me to have a sandwich. And he waited until 6 o'clock in the morning. And he put me in a train who goes to [? Terezina. ?] When you found your brother there, what did you and your brother-- where did-- what did you do then? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to this village. This is a village. And this is what-- a [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and it was very hard to go in because this was a quarantine that time-- typhus and-- and dysentery. And I have the cigarettes. And I said to this Czech-- the first was Czech. The second one, closer to the-- to the-- to the-- go in to the fence close, very close. And he said yes. So I gave him a pack of cigarettes, a fortune. I didn't know it cost so much. And he said, listen, now is the nice guy there. You can go to him. Give him a cigarette, not the whole package.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to myself, what he's talking about? What is a package cigarette? Nothing. That time, it cost very little in Poland-- cigarettes. And I gave him the pack of cigarettes. And I told him, I have a brother. How do you know you have a brother? I said, if you would have somebody, you would find out. You know, you will find out. So I came in. And I saw the young people. He was 16 years old at that time. He looked like a-- a shadow, a old man, you know, when they are completely sick? Like, he was very sick. So I went to the doctor. He said, we don't have anything else to do. He has to rest. I said, can I stay with him? He said, no. I said, I was sick. I had dysentery. I had typhus. I didn't have it. And I want to stay with him. So I take care of the boys with-- I-- they brought the cans of soup for them. So I gave those soup. They couldn't eat the soup. When I tell them stories about soup, they-- they ate. And I washed the can.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a stove there. And I put-- they have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- I think they-- they heat the-- the stove of-- to make warm, because it was still cold. It was in-- I went there. It was just, I think, in May. And I was with him. And I bathed him every day. And he had lice in the skin. First time in my life I saw something like that. And I gave him baths every day. And every day I saw that he's better and better. And then I found out that that boy who is with him-- he was not so sick like him-- is feeling much better. And he's not so sick like my brother. So we took him out from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I stopped by a-- before going to the-- to the-- to the camp where they were-- were sitting there-- to a peasant there. And I said, can I have something to wash myself? And I gave him also cigarettes. And he gave me a piece of-- of soap.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he watched-- the-- the fence are make of stones that nobody can see from the outside what's on the inside. I-- I didn't care if he see me naked or not. I washed myself. I was dirty because of three days of waiting to go to the-- from one train to the other train. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went. And I took this other boy too, because I find out that's-- his other-- the-- his mother is in Czestochowa. And I told him, you know, your mother is in Czestochowa, and she's going to be very happy to see you. So he-- both boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the cigarettes, I used to go every day out to the peasant and buy carrots and make juice of it and gave it to my brother. And a-- a little by little, he gain a little bit of appetite. But he was still very sick. He couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't tell me about my father, I think-- nothing. And I didn't ask him. I didn't want to ask him in the beginning. And I took him to the railroad station. And I told him that the boys are in the wagon there. This was people going to work from [? Terezina ?] to another town. And they gave me the compartment. And they-- both boys were laying on this thing-- very nice. And every stop, they came-- the Czech came over. And they bring something to drink or something to eat, whatever you-- you-- you desire, you know, to eat or to drink or something. And we came to Prague. We came to Prague. The HIAS-- not the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a hospital, American hospital. Not the HIAS. What was the other one? Ooh.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Don't remember. I put my brother and this boy to the hospital. They were two weeks, and I went home to Czestochowa to tell my mother that he is alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have to go back in two weeks to take him home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I took him home in two weeks. But still he was-- was swollen, completely swollen. After you see where you are completely-- very starved-- starved. The beginning, you look like a skeleton. And later on, when you get better, you became so swollen up. And he can not-- the-- the train was full of people. You didn't pay-- you didn't buy tickets for the thing. Everything was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked some-- some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conductor there where he was-- not a conductor, somebody who take care of the train-- to let me go and for the-- two boys has to be on the top because they cannot breathe. They're sick. And he said, but it's very dangerous because of the Russians there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was. So a-- a Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] He is.
